coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous detection of multiple microRNAs based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer under a single excitation wavelength
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

He, Jia Hui

Liu, Hui
Li, Chun Mei
coredata.dc:description: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play a key role in physiological processes, and their dysregulation is closely related to various human diseases. Simultaneous detection of multiple miRNAs is pivotal to cancer diagnosis at an early stage. However, most multicomponent analyses generally involve multiple excitation wavelengths, which are complicated and often challenging to simultaneously acquire multiple detection signals. In this study, a convenient and sensitive sensor was developed to simultaneously detection of multiple miRNAs under a single excitation wavelength through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the carbon dots (CDs)/quantum dots (QDs) and graphene oxide (GO). A hybridization chain reaction (HCR) was triggered by miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, resulting in the high sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 50 pM (3σ/k) for miRNA-141 and 60 pM (3σ/k) for miRNA-21. This simultaneous assay also showed excellent specificity discrimination against the mismatch. Furthermore, our proposed method successfully detected miRNA-21 and miRNA-141 in human serum samples at a same time, indicating its diagnostic potential in a clinical setting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simultaneous detection
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Multiple microRNAs
Carbon dot
Quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, CD-DNA (H2), and QDs-DNA (H4)
Detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in human serum samples
Principle for the simultaneously fluorescent detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21
Characterization of CDs, CD-DNA (H2), and QD-DNA (H4)
Feasibility of miRNA detection using fluorescence
Sensitivity and specificity for the detection of miRNA
Detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-prepared CD-DNA (H2) and QD-DNA (H4). (a) The zeta potentials of CDs, DNA (H2), and CD-DNA (H2). (b) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis of the prepared CDs-DNA (H2). (c) Zeta potentials of QDs, DNA (H4), and QD-DNA (H4). (d) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis of the prepared QDs-DNA (H4).

Feasibility analysis. (a) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis (lanes 1–11, DNA marker, miRNA-141, DNA (H1), CD-DNA (H2), DNA (H1) + CD-DNA (H2), miRNA-141 + DNA (H1) + CD-DNA (H2), miRNA-21, DNA (H3), QD-DNA (H4), DNA (H3) + QD-DNA(H4), miRNA-21 + DNA (H3) + QD-DNA(H4). (b) and (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs and QDs with different concentrations of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, respectively. Experimental conditions: H1 and H3, 200 nM; CDs-DNA, 10 μL; QDs-DNA, 20 μL; GO, 125 μg/mL.

Optimization of experimental conditions. Effect of (a) the concentration of GO, (b) incubation temperature, and (c) incubation time, (d) quenching time with GO. F/F 0, where F is the fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of target miRNA-141 or miRNA-21, and F 0 is the fluorescence emission intensity in the absence of target miRNA-141 or miRNA-21. Experimental conditions: miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, 7.5 nM; H1 and H3, 200 nM; CDs-DNA, 10 μL; QDs-DNA, 20 μL; GO, 125 μg/mL.

Sensitivity in detecting miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 by our method. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentrations of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 ranging from 0 to 10 nM (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 nM); (b) A linear relationship between F/F 0 and the concentrations of miRNA-141 or miRNA-21. (c) A linear relationship between F/F 0 and miRNA-141 in the absence of miRNA-21. (d) A linear relationship between F/F 0 and miRNA-21 in the absence of miRNA-141. Experimental conditions: H1 and H3, 200 nM; CDs-DNA, 10 μL; QDs-DNA, 20 μL; GO, 125 µg/mL.

Selectivity in detecting miRNA-141/miRNA-21 by our method. Experimental conditions: concentrations of Mis-0, Mis-2, Mis-4, Mis-5, and miRNA-145 were 7.5 nM; H1 and H3, 200 nM; CDs-DNA, 10 μL; QDs-DNA, 20 μL; GO, 125 μg/mL.

(a) Illustration of the simultaneous detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 based on FRET using a single excitation wavelength. (b) Images of CDs and QDs using visible light (left) and ultraviolet light (right) at 365 nm. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and QDs. Excitation spectra of CDs (blue line), emission spectra of CDs and QDs with miRNA-141/miRNA-21 (purple line), and emission spectra of CDs and QDs without miRNA-141/miRNA-21 (gray line).
Determination of miRNA in human serum (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Simultaneous detection of multiple microRNAs based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer under a single excitation wavelength

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NMPA Key Laboratory for Quality Monitoring of Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substance, Chongqing 401121, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics (Southwest University), Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics (Southwest University)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lin Liu and Jia Hui He contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simultaneous detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 using a single excitation wavelength was reported in this work based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots/quantum dots and graphene oxide.

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play a key role in physiological processes, and their dysregulation is closely related to various human diseases. Simultaneous detection of multiple miRNAs is pivotal to cancer diagnosis at an early stage. However, most multicomponent analyses generally involve multiple excitation wavelengths, which are complicated and often challenging to simultaneously acquire multiple detection signals. In this study, a convenient and sensitive sensor was developed to simultaneously detection of multiple miRNAs under a single excitation wavelength through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the carbon dots (CDs)/quantum dots (QDs) and graphene oxide (GO). A hybridization chain reaction (HCR) was triggered by miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, resulting in the high sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 50 pM (3σ/k) for miRNA-141 and 60 pM (3σ/k) for miRNA-21. This simultaneous assay also showed excellent specificity discrimination against the mismatch. Furthermore, our proposed method successfully detected miRNA-21 and miRNA-141 in human serum samples at a same time, indicating its diagnostic potential in a clinical setting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are endogenous non-coding single-stranded small RNAs, ranging from 19 to 23 nucleotides in length, crucially involved in the regulation of gene expression [1,2]. Over one-third of human genes are regulated by miRNA, some of which connect to hundreds of target genes [3,4]. Variations in the expression levels of miRNA are associated with numerous human diseases. For instance, several research have demonstrated miRNA-21 and miRNA-141 were highly expressed in early and late stages of lung adenocarcinoma patients including smokers and non-smokers, suggesting their potential as biomarkers for early detection of lung cancer [5]. Conventional approaches for the quantitative determination of miRNA encompass Northern blotting [6], real-time fluorescence quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) [7], and microarray [8], which usually suffer from drawbacks such as sophisticated analytical instruments, intricate procedural steps, time-intensive protocols, and limited sensitivity [9]. Currently, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), electrochemical analysis and fluorescence analysis have been developed to detect miRNA, which are preferred over the existing Conventional approaches in terms of convenience, reaction time and cost-effectiveness [10–13]. Among the above, the fluorescence method stands out owing to the advantages of simple apparatus setup, low cost, good quantitative feasibility, excellent reproducibility, and high sensitivity, driving rapid advancement [14]. Of greater importance, the fluorescence method is simple, fast, and easy to operate when contrasted with other analytical methodologies.[15]. In addition, to further enhance the detection sensitivity of miRNA, diverse signal amplification strategies, such as rolling circuit amplification (RCA) [16,17], strand displacement amplification (SDA) [18,19], catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) [20,21] and hybridization chain reaction (HCR) [22,23] have been integrated with existing methods.

Being integral to fluorescence analysis, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a simple and sensitive analytical method [24,25]. FRET-based methods combined with HCR signal-amplification strategies have found extensive applications in miRNA detection [26,27]. For example, a FRET-based HCR strategy was developed for the analysis of miRNA-1246 utilizing Cy3 as donor and Cy5 as acceptor [28]. A light-controlled recruitable HCR and FRET between FAM and TAMRA was combined for monitoring and imaging miRNA-21 expression in living cells [29]. Typically, these FRET methods employing fluorescent organic molecules suffer from poor resistance to photobleaching, pH sensitivity, and biological toxicity [30]. In contrast, carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots (QDs) are ideal energy donors because of their ease of preparation, excellent photostability, tunable emission wavelengths, and the presence of abundant functional groups [31–34]. Consequently, the FRET based on either CDs or QDs has found broad application in bioanalysis [35,36], bioimaging [37,38], and medical diagnosis [39,40]. However, the employment of these methods for the detection of single miRNA in clinical settings may yield false-positive results [41,42]. Thus, the simultaneous detection of multiple miRNAs can enhance the accuracy of disease diagnosis. For example, a metal–organic frameworks-based CHA platform was constructed for simultaneous sensitive determination of acute myocardial infarction related microRNAs [43]. A pentagon DNA nanostructure was created to simultaneously image miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 in living cells with FAM and Cy5 as signal molecules [44]. However, FRET-based accurate detection of multi-targets primarily requires multi-wavelength excitation, which makes the operation cumbersome. Hence, it is essential to devise a simple method that excited by a single wavelength to simultaneously detect multiple miRNAs, thereby circumventing the complexity [45,46].

In this study, utilizing CDs and QDs as energy donors and graphene oxide (GO) as an energy acceptor, we report a FRET analytical approach with dual signals under a single excitation to achieve concurrent detection of dual miRNA. The emission peaks of CDs and QDs didn’t cause interference with each other in the fluorescence spectrum under a single excitation. As a result, a single wavelength enabled simultaneous excitation of QDs and CDs, eliminating the need for cumbersome multi-wavelength switching. Additionally, GO exhibited the properties of fluorescence quenching and DNA adsorption, which avoided the additional quenching groups and time-consuming covalent modifications with DNA strands, further simplifying the simultaneous detection approach. The design includes the following four sequences: hairpin DNA (H1), amino-modified hairpin DNA (H2), DNA (H3), and biotin-modified hairpin DNA (H4). Among these, H2 and H4 were coupled on carboxyl-rich CDs and streptavidin-modified QDs, respectively. When lacking miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, the hairpin DNA was adsorbed onto GO, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence of CDs and QDs by GO through FRET. Interestingly, miRNA-141 could initiate the HCR between DNA (H1) and CD-DNA (H2), whereas miRNA-21 could induce the HCR between DNA (H3) and QDs-DNA (H4), which caused the detachment of DNA hairpin from the GO surface. Under these circumstances, FRET became off. The fluorescence intensity of CDs and QDs gradually restored with increasing concentrations of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, providing a sensitive detection approach with a single excitation wavelength of CDs, as depicted in Scheme 1 .

Spermine was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany) and citric acid was sourced from Aladdin Biochemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GO was procured from Xianfeng Nanomaterials Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Quantum dot-labeled streptavidin-605 (SA-QDs) was acquired from Jiayuan Quantum Company (Wuhan, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (20 × PBS), ribonuclease inhibitor, and water treated diethyl pyrocarbonate (DEPC) were purchased from Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All DNA and RNA sequences employed in this study were synthesized and purified by ShengGong Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The detailed DNA and RNA sequences provided in Table S1. All DNA sequences underwent a 5-minute annealing process at 95 °C and were subsequently stored at room temperature for 30 min before application. All solutions were prepared with DEPC-treated water.

The F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) were empolyed to record fluorescence (FL) spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were conducted using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN microscope (FEI, USA). A Zetasizer Nano ZS System (Malvern) was utilized for measuring zeta potential, and an FL-TCSPC spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) was utilized for determining the fluorescence lifetime. Agarose gel electrophoresis was carried out with the DYCZ-24DN electrophoresis unit (Liuyi, Beijing, China), and the gel was imaged under UV light by the BG-gdsAUTO520 (Baygene, Beijing, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, CD-DNA (H2), and QDs-DNA (H4)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared following our earlier published work [47]. Simply put, 0.05 g spermine and 0.10 g citric acid were thoroughly blended in 5 mL water, further the mixture was moved to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and maintain at 160 °C for 3 h. The synthesis of CD-DNA (H2) followed the protocol outlined in the report from Guo’s group [48]. Briefly, the prepared CDs were initially dissolved in 5 mL of 1 × PBS buffer to form a CD suspension (0.1 mg/mL), which was then adjusted to pH 5 to protonate the carboxyl groups of the CDs. Subsequently, a mixture of 19 mg EDC and 22 mg NHS was incorporated to the CD suspension and agitated for 30 min at ambient temperature to activate the carboxylic groups of the CDs. Then, the CD suspension was adjusted to pH 7, and proceed with the addition of 24 μL amino-modified DNA (H2, 100 μM) under continuous stirring for 2 h at 25 °C to facilitate condensation reaction. The CD-DNA (H2) conjugate solution was preserve overnight at 4 °C, followed by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 20 min using ultrafiltration centrifuge tubes. The resultant CD-DNA (H2) conjugate solution was kept in the dark at 4 °C until needed. QD-DNA (H4) was produced by combining 30 nM SA-QD solution with 200 nM biotin-DNA (H4) solution in 1 × PBS buffer and allowing the reaction to proceed for 1 h.

Ten microliters each of 4 μM H1 and H3 solutions were mixed with 10 μL of CD-DNA (H2) and 20 μL of QD-DNA (H4) in 1 × PBS, followed by the addition of 20 U of enzyme inhibitors. Then, 10 μL of miRNA-141 and 10 μL of miRNA-21 with varying concentrations were incorporated into the aforementioned solutions maintained on ice. The solution was vortexed well and maintained at 37 °C for 3.5 h, followed by the addition of GO solution and incubation at 25 °C for 15 min. The final reaction solution was stimulated at 360 nm by fluorescence spectrophotometry, and the emission peaks at 466 nm (for CDs) and 612 nm (for QDs) were obtained. The selectivity for miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 was confirmed by reaction with other miRNAs instead of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, using the identical experimental protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum was utilized as the subject of this experiment to validate the practicality and suitability of miRNA analysis by our method. Serum samples were sourced from volunteers at Southwest University Hospital, Chongqing, China. All experimental procedures were conducted in compliance with the pertinent laws and the institutional guidelines of ethical standards of the institutional committee of Southwest University (approval number yxy2021125). The serum samples were spiked with standard solutions of varying concentrations of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 (0.5, 3, and 6 nM) and analyzed using our proposed method. The processed spiked serum samples were incubated with 10 μL of 4 μM H1 and H3, 10 μL of CD-DNA (H2), 20 μL of QD-DNA (H4), and 20 U enzyme inhibitor at 37 °C for 3.5 h. Then, 125 µg/mL GO solution was added for further incubation at 25 °C for 15 min. The fluorescence intensity at 466 nm (CDs) and 612 nm (QDs) was measured from the final reaction solution under excitation at 360 nm, followed by the calculation of recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle for the simultaneously fluorescent detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of dual-miRNA detection using HCR and FRET between CDs/QDs and GO was illustrated in Scheme 1a. The experimental components comprised hairpin DNA (H1), CD-DNA (H2), hairpin DNA (H3), QD-DNA (H4), and GO. The detailed sequence design and reaction process was illustrated in Fig. S1. Without miRNA-141 and miRNA-21, catalytic DNA circuits failed to be triggered. In such cases, hairpin DNA was adsorbed by GO duo to hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions, causing the fluorescence quenching of CDs and QDs. The presence of miRNA-141 could induce the opening of hairpin H1, which exposed sticky end capable of binding with hairpin CD-DNA (H2), thereby driving the dynamic self-assembly of miRNA-141, H1, and CD-DNA (H2). In the same manner, miRNA-21 could also trigger the opening of hairpin H3, subsequently allowing the sticky end of hairpin H3 to open QD-DNA (H4), resulting to the dynamic self-assembly of miRNA-21, H3, and QD-DNA (H4). Due to the weak interaction, the extended double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) structures formed by these reactions detaches from GO, causing the fluorescence recovery of the CDs and QDs. Furthermore, upon excitation with 360 nm UV light, the CDs and QDs could emit vivid blue and red fluorescence (Scheme 1b, c). Hence, it is capable to sensitively detect both miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 simultaneously through the utilization of a single excitation wavelength to generate the fluorescence of the CDs and QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, CD-DNA (H2), and QD-DNA (H4)
ce:section.ce:para: First, we characterized the synthesized CDs and the coupled CD-DNA (H2) and QD-DNA (H4). In our previous study [47], we had successfully obtained CDs enriched with carboxylic groups, which conferred remarkable water solubility as well as function as reaction groups for condensation with the amino-modified DNA. The successful modification of DNA (H2) on the surface of the as-prepared CDs was identified by the presence of the prominent absorption peak centered at 260 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of CD-DNA (Fig. S2). Zeta potentials of the CDs, DNA (H2), and CD-DNA (H2) were determined to be −6.5 mV, −4.0 mV, and −17.1 mV, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The surface charge of CDs became further negative upon the attachment of DNA, providing evidence of the successful connection between CDs and DNA (H2). Additionally, on the basis of the results of agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. 1b), CDs-DNA (H2) moved lower than DNA (H2) but faster than CDs. This is because CDs possessed a relatively low negative charge, while CDs-DNA (H2) modified with extensive DNA (H2) had a higher negative charge, leading to charge-dominated mobility differences [49]. The result of electrophores was further confirming the successful binding of DNA on the surface of the CDs.

The coupling of QDs and DNA (H4) was also evidenced in a manner identical to that of CDs-DNA (H2). The alteration in zeta potential values suggested that the surface charge of QDs shifted from −2.6 mV to −16.7 mV following coupling with biotin-DNA (Fig. 1c). As depicted in Fig. 1d, QDs-DNA(H4) with a high negative charge exhibited a higher electrophoretic mobility compared to individual QDs with a low negative charge, which can be explained with the same reason as CDs-DNA(H2) [50]. As a consequence, both the zeta potential values and the outcomes of agarose gel electrophoresis confirmed the successful coupling of biotin-DNA (H4) onto the surface of the SA-QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of miRNA detection using fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming to verify the feasibility of the proposed method, the fluorescence intensities of CDs and QDs was assessed both in the absence and presence of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21. Additionally, agarose gel electrophoresis was conducted and the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs and QDs was carried out. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs and QDs significantly enhanced in the presence of miRNA-141 or miRNA-21, as opposed to when neither miRNA-141 nor miRNA-21 was present (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 2 a, when miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 were absent, almost no dsDNA was formed (lanes 5, 10), whereas distinct dsDNA bands were observed in the presence of miRNA-141 or miRNA-21 (lanes 6, 11). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs reduced from 9.05 ns to 2.87 ns owing to the fluorescence quenching by GO, which could gradually returned to 6.31 ns with 5 nM miRNA-141 (Fig. 2b, Table S2). Likewise, the fluorescence lifetime of QDs could be dropped from 33.30 ns to 8.62 ns upon the addition of GO, and rose to 17.70 ns following adding 5 nM miRNA-21 (Fig. 2c, Table S2). Thus, it could be inferred that the proposed sensor platform based on HCR and FRET between CDs/QDs and GO was feasible and sensitive for detecting miRNA-141 and miRNA-21.

To bolster the analytical performance, we initially examined the influence of GO concentrations. Fig. 3 a demonstrated the a steadily rise in fluorescence ratio (F/F 0) as the GO concentration transitioned from 25 to 125 μg/mL. Such augmentation may stem from the adsorption/desorption of the dsDNA structure on GO. Nevertheless, an excessive amount of GO could quench the fluorescence of the CDs/QDs considerably, leading to a decline in F/F 0. Thus, the optimal concentration for GO was determined to be 125 µg/mL. Subsequently, the impact of the incubation temperature on the DNA self-assembly with the hairpins were explored. Incubation temperature serves as an essential role in reaction kinetics, affecting the stability and interaction of the hairpin probes. The formation of dsDNA structure complexes was lower at low incubation temperatures owing to the weaker molecular collisions. Higher incubation temperatures made a higher likelihood of probe collisions, resulting in the more formation of the self-assembled products. As seen in Fig. 3b, Selecting 37 °C as the optimum incubation temperature according to the maximum F/F 0 value. Afterwards, we delved into the impact of reaction time on the HCR to optimize the performance of this probe. In most cases, a longer reaction time is conducive to a more thorough response. But when the reaction is at equilibrium, further increasing the time is futile. So we optimized the incubation time within 30–210 min range. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the F/F 0 gradually reached its peak at 180 min and remained stable even at 210 min with growth in incubation time. Consequently, 210 min was determined as the best incubation time. Additionally, the incubation time for GO (quenching time) was optimized as well. The F/F 0 peaked at 15 min and decreased gradually with longer quenching time (Fig. 3d). Thus, 15 min was identified as the optimum quenching time. We found that the optimized experimental conditions for the detection of both miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 were similar, offering feasibility for their simultaneous detection under a similar set of conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and specificity for the detection of miRNA
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the analytical performance of the fluorescence detection platform devised in our study, various concentrations of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 were determined under the best experimental conditions. As seen in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence signals gradually recovered with an increase in the miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 concentrations. F/F 0 exhibited a favorable linear relationship with miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in the 0.1–10 nM range. The linear equations for the detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 were F/F 0 = 0.203 c miRNA-141 + 1.42 and F/F 0 = 0.209 c miRNA-21 + 1.16, with the correlation coefficients (r 2) of 0.993 and 0.992 (Fig. 4b). In addition, the limits of determination (LOD) were 50 pM and 60 pM (3σ/k), respectively. In comparison to other reported works, our approach displayed comparable or even superior sensitivity in detecting miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 (Tables S3 and S4). To investigate whether there were differences between the detection of multiple and individual miRNAs, we conducted studies using only one miRNA at a time. When only miRNA-141 or miRNA-21 was present, there were robust linear correlations between the F/F 0 and the concentration of miRNA-141 or miRNA-21 ranging from 0.1 to 10 nM, described in Fig. 4c-d with the linear equation of F/F 0 = 0.228 c miRNA-141 + 1.38 (r 2 = 0.997) or F/F 0 = 0.236 c miRNA-21 + 1.61 (r 2 = 0.985), respectively. These findings suggested that our method was suitable for the simultaneous multi-targets determination of miRNAs without interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming to validate the practical utility and accuracy of the developed array in complex biological samples, we attempted to quantify miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 in human serum samples. A recovery experiment was conducted to verify the accuracy and precision of our method. The serum samples was analyzed by standard addition method following the addition of 0.5, 3, 6 nM miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 to the samples. The mean recoveries in the six samples for both miRNAs were in the range of 90.0–103.7 % (Table 1 ), indicating the accuracy of our probe and its suitability in analyzing biological samples.

In summarize, a sensitive fluorescence method based on FRET and HCR was devised, enabling simultaneous detection of multiple miRNAs with a single excitation wavelength. The existence of miRNA-141 and miRNA-21 could induce the dynamic self-assembly of DNA, thereby forming longer dsDNA. Based on weak binding interactions between the dsDNA and GO, the further fluorescence-based recovery of CDs and QDs was achieved by utilizing a single excitation wavelength of CDs. In contrast to single miRNAs detection, simultaneous detection of multiple miRNAs can not only effectively avoid false-positive outcomes but also enhance the accuracy of disease diagnosis. Thus, these findings implied the promise of our approach for disease monitoring and treatment in a clinical setting.

Lin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jia Hui He: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiao Qiao Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jia Jun Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Wen Yi Lv: Validation, Formal analysis. Cheng Zhi Huang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Hui Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chun Mei Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 22074124 and 21976144), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0521) and Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (No. cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0178).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124788.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MicroRNAs in kidney injury and disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Mahtal, O. Lenoir, C. Tinel, et al., MicroRNAs in kidney injury and disease, Nat. Rev. Nephrol. 18 (2022) 643-662, doi: 10.1038/s41581-022-00608-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The biochemical basis of microRNA targeting efficacy
